# HDS — llms-full.txt

Single-page, agent-readable summary of Health Data Safe (HDS): what it is,
how its data model works, and the three concrete ways to act on a user's
account (MCP server, `hds-lib` JavaScript library, raw REST). If you are an
AI agent helping a user with HDS, read this once at session start.

Canonical URL: https://agents.datasafe.dev/llms-full.txt
(moved 2026-06-11 from docs.datasafe.dev/developers/llms-full.txt — update
your pointers; the old URL serves a redirect note)
Last updated: 2026-06-19 (itemDef-first rewritten as an end-to-end
provision→write→read rule with StreamsAutoCreate + getNecessaryListForItems;
anti-pattern box; custom-itemDef-via-overload path; multi-item worked
example §5b-multi; coach↔patient sharing §5e; events.get read trap + root
provisioning sharp edges §7; browser-app session-persistence guidance.
2026-06-12: single feedback tracker + GitHub signup walkthrough; pryv/lib-js
doc pointer. 2026-06-11: moved to agents.datasafe.dev; development
permissions guidance, structured-data-over-notes rule, feedback channel)

Feedback: if you or your user hit a problem with these instructions, file
a GitHub issue at https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues —
agents may draft the issue text for the user. This is the SINGLE tracker
for all agent-related issues (MCP bugs, data-model gaps, docs problems);
do not file on other healthdatasafe repos — maintainers route internally.
If the user has no GitHub account, guide them through the free signup at
https://github.com/signup (email + username + password + emailed launch
code), then to
https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues/new/choose.

---

## 1. What HDS is

Health Data Safe is an open infrastructure for personal health data, built
on top of Pryv (https://pryv.com). A user owns their HDS account, holds the
authoritative token to it, and grants per-app access via OAuth. Apps and
bridges read/write structured events into the user's account; viewer apps
compose those events across sources.

| Pointer                          | URL                                                   |
|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| Site                             | https://datasafe.dev                                  |
| Foundation                       | https://www.healthdatasafe.org                        |
| Agents site (this page's home)   | https://agents.datasafe.dev                           |
| Developer docs                   | https://docs.datasafe.dev/developers/                 |
| Feedback (GitHub issues)         | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues  |
| Data model (machine-readable)    | https://model.datasafe.dev/pack.json                  |
| Data model browser (human)       | https://model-browser.datasafe.dev                    |
| Style/theme                      | https://style.datasafe.dev                            |
| GitHub org                       | https://github.com/healthdatasafe                     |

### Environments

| Alias | service-info URL                            | API host pattern   |
|-------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| demo  | https://demo.datasafe.dev/reg/service/info  | demo.datasafe.dev/{username}/ |
| prod  | https://reg.api.datasafe.dev/service/info   | *.api.datasafe.dev |

Default for any agent flow is **demo**. Production writes are gated off in
the MCP server until the operator opts in.

---

## 2. The data model — events, streams, items

Three primitives:

- **Event** — a single data point: `{ id, time, duration?, streamIds[], type, content, tags? }`.
  `time` is a Unix timestamp (seconds, float). `type` is a Pryv string in the
  form `category/unit` (e.g. `mass/kg`, `temperature/c`, `note/txt`).
  `content` shape depends on `type`.
- **Stream** — a container for events. Streams form a tree (`parentId`).
  A user organises events by attaching them to one or more streams.
- **Item** (HDS-specific) — the canonical, human-meaningful unit of health
  observation. An item declares the canonical `streamId` + `eventType` +
  content shape for one kind of observation (body-weight,
  basal-body-temperature, cervical-fluid-observation, …). Items are the
  cross-system layer — they map to HealthKit, FHIR, SNOMED CT, ICF, PROMIS,
  EQ-5D anchors so the same observation has a consistent meaning across
  apps and sources.

### Catalogue size (as of 2026-06-10 — query pack.json for current numbers)

~100 items, 12 top-level streams, 2 converters. `pack.eventTypes` is an
object `{ types, extras }`: `types` is the full event-type catalogue
(~380 entries — Pryv's standard types merged with HDS-specific ones;
each entry: `{ description, type }` content schema), `extras` carries
display metadata per type (localised name + unit symbol, e.g.
`{ name: { en: "Grays" }, symbol: "Gy" }`).

### Item categories

One YAML file per category in `data-model/definitions/items/`:

`activity`, `body`, `body-skin`, `body-vulva`, `family`, `fertility`,
`function` (ICF/EQ-5D), `medication`, `nutrition`, `procedure`, `profile`,
`symptom`, `treatment`, `wellbeing`.

### Item pack — the machine-readable manifest

The flattened, agent-ready bundle is published at
https://model.datasafe.dev/pack.json. Top-level keys: `publicationDate,
streams, items, eventTypes, datasources, conversions, converters,
settings, appStreams`.

`items` is an object keyed by item key. Two real entries, illustrating the
two shape variants:

**Simple — has a base `eventType`** (`pack.items["body-temperature-basal"]`):

```json
{
  "version": "v1",
  "label": { "en": "Basal Body Temperature", "fr": "Température basale" },
  "description": { "en": "Resting body temperature taken on waking…" },
  "streamId": "body-temperature-basal",
  "eventType": "temperature/c",
  "type": "number",
  "repeatable": "unlimited"
}
```

**Variations-only — no base `eventType`** (`pack.items["body-weight"]`):

```json
{
  "label": { "en": "Body weight" },
  "streamId": "body-weight",
  "type": "number",
  "variations": {
    "eventType": {
      "label": { "en": "Unit" },
      "options": [
        { "value": "mass/kg", "label": { "en": "Kg" } },
        { "value": "mass/lb", "label": { "en": "Lbs" } }
      ]
    }
  },
  "repeatable": "unlimited"
}
```

When `variations.eventType` is present and there is **no top-level
`eventType`**, the agent must pick a value from `options[]`. Default to the
first option (metric — `mass/kg` for weight, `length/m` for height) unless
the user explicitly asked for imperial. **If the user's input is
unit-ambiguous** ("log my weight as 158") **ask before defaulting** —
silently picking kg-vs-lb is a real data-quality risk.

Field meanings:

- `streamId` — canonical Pryv stream for events of this item.
- `eventType` / `variations.eventType` — the Pryv `category/unit` string.
- `type` — content schema family (`number`, `string`, `object`, `null`, …).
  ⚠ Same word, two meanings: an **item's** `type` is this content-shape
  family; an **event's** `type` is the Pryv `category/unit` string (the
  item's `eventType`). When building an event, `event.type` ←
  `item.eventType`, never `item.type`.
- `repeatable` — `unlimited` for time-series; some items are one-shot.
- `hooks` (optional) — semantic anchors mapping the value to ICF / EQ-5D /
  PROMIS / HealthKit scales for cross-system compatibility.
- `deprecated: true` — readable but should not be surfaced for new writes.

### Accesses & permissions

Every API call runs under an **access** (the token in the apiEndpoint).
What an access can touch is a list of per-stream **permissions**, each
with a level:

- `read` — read events in the stream.
- `contribute` — read + create/modify events (what data-writing flows need).
- `manage` — contribute + create/modify sub-streams.

Permissions are **stream-scoped and inherit down the stream tree**;
`streamId: "*"` means the whole account. Access kinds: `personal` (the
user's own full-account token), `app` (what OAuth gives an app — scoped
to the permissions the user approved on the consent screen), `shared`
(scoped, revocable links the user hands out — e.g. a patient sharing
read-only streams with a coach; full mint→consume→list→revoke lifecycle in
§5e). A `forbidden` (HTTP 403) error on a write
usually means the access level is too low (e.g. `read` where
`contribute` was needed) — re-auth with the right level instead of
retrying. The MCP's `connect` requests `streamId: "*"` ("All HDS data")
at the chosen level (`contribute` by default); per-stream scoping is
roadmap. Apps built with `hds-lib` should request only the streams they
need (see §5b). Reference:
https://pryv.github.io/reference/#access

### App stream convention

Apps and bridges that produce source-contextual data (notes, chat, sync
metadata) use an "app stream" pattern to separate app-specific content
from universal HDS data:

```
{app-id}/                        BASE — root stream for the bridge/app
├── {app-id}-raw/                RAW — synced source data
│   ├── {app-id}-raw-new
│   ├── {app-id}-raw-error
│   └── {app-id}-raw-converted
└── {app-id}-app/                APP — app-contextual content
    └── {app-id}-app-notes       free text notes from this source
```

The access token created for each app declares its app stream in
`clientData.appStreamId`, so viewer apps can group source-contextual notes
by the originating app. App streams use standard event types
(`note/txt`, `message/...`) — no custom HDS items needed for app-contextual
content.

---

## 3. The itemDef-first rule — items drive provision, write, AND read

Items are not just a write-time lookup. They are the **single source of
truth** for which streams an app creates, how it writes events, and which
streams it reads back. Whenever the user asks the agent to record, import,
or read health data:

```
1. LOOK UP   the canonical HDS item(s) for the data
2. PROVISION the streams those item(s) need — derived from the model, parents first
3. WRITE     events using the item's streamId + eventType (+ unit, on variations)
4. READ      back by querying the SAME model-derived stream set
```

Skipping the lookup and inventing `streamId`/`eventType` strings — or
hand-maintaining your own stream tree (`id` / `parentId` / `name`) copied
out of the pack — breaks the cross-system mapping items carry **and**
silently drifts from the canonical model the moment either side changes.
**Always look up first; derive streams and event shapes from the model,
never hand-write them. On a genuine miss, define your own itemDef (below)
or ask the user — never invent loose strings.**

> ### ⛔ Anti-pattern — the #1 mistake new HDS apps make
>
> Do **NOT** hardcode a stream tree, parent chain, stream names, or
> `eventType` strings in app code. The first two apps built on HDS both did
> exactly this — copying streamIds/eventTypes from the pack into app
> constants — and both hit the same two failures: (1) the hand-built tree
> drifted from the model as soon as the model changed, and (2) reads
> silently returned **zero** events, because a single unknown stream in an
> `events.get` filter fails the *whole* query (see §7). Derive everything
> from the model, keyed off the item keys your app uses. In `hds-lib`:
>
> ```js
> const APP_ITEM_KEYS = ['body-weight', 'body-temperature-basal', 'cervical-fluid-observation'];
> const model = HDSLib.getHDSModel();
>
> // PROVISION — ONE call creates every stream these items need (parents first, idempotent).
> // You do NOT assemble the batch yourself:
> await HDSLib.toolkit.StreamsAutoCreate.attachToConnection(connection).ensureExistsForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS);
>
> // WRITE — never hand-write streamId/eventType:
> const tmpl = model.itemsDefs.forKey('body-weight').eventTemplate();  // { streamIds, type }
>
> // READ — derive the events.get `streams` filter from the SAME item set:
> const streamIds = model.streams.getNecessaryListForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS, { knowExistingStreamsIds: [] }).map(s => s.id);
> ```
>
> (`getNecessaryListForItems` is the lower-level primitive — it returns the
> raw `{ id, name, defaultName?, parentId }` payloads if you ever need them.
> `StreamsAutoCreate.ensureExistsForItems` is what the two early apps
> reimplemented by hand; use it instead.)
>
> MCP and REST users derive the same structure from `pack.json` — read
> `pack.items[key].streamId` and walk `pack.streams` for parents; never
> transcribe the tree into code.

**Structured data over notes — push back on `note/txt`.** When the user
brings a dataset HDS has no item for (an unbridged app's export, a
spreadsheet, a tracker CSV), do NOT dump it into `note/txt` events if the
information can be structured. Notes are opaque: they cannot be charted,
converted, compared across sources, or mapped to HealthKit/FHIR/SNOMED CT.
Investigation order: (1) `search_items` / pack.items for an existing item;
(2) the event-type catalogue (`pack.eventTypes`) for a fitting
`category/unit` type; (3) surface the gap to the user and recommend filing
an issue at https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues so the
data model can grow a proper item; (4) only genuinely free-form content (a
diary entry, a letter's text) belongs in `note/txt`. Push back even if the
user asks for notes as the easy way out — explain what they would lose.

### No item exists yet? Define your own — don't hardcode, don't dump to notes

When HDS has **no** item for the data, there is a supported third path that
keeps you on the itemDef-first rule instead of falling back to raw
streamId/eventType or `note/txt`: **load your own itemDef into `hds-lib`.**
`initHDSModel({ overload })` accepts an overload the model merges in; adding
a brand-new item (plus the streams and eventTypes it needs) is always
allowed:

```js
await HDSLib.initHDSModel({
  overload: {
    items: {
      'body-my-observation': {
        label: { en: 'My observation' },
        streamId: 'body-my-observation',
        eventType: 'note/txt',      // or a fitting category/unit from pack.eventTypes
        type: 'string'
      }
    },
    // declare the stream under an EXISTING root (here: body) — see the root caveat below
    streams: [{ id: 'body-my-observation', name: 'My observation', parentId: 'body' }]
  }
});
// From here the custom item behaves like any core item — same itemDef-first API:
const item = HDSLib.getHDSModel().itemsDefs.forKey('body-my-observation');
const tmpl = item.eventTemplate();
```

Overriding an *existing* item via overload is limited to display fields
(`label/description/repeatable/reminder/devNotes`); structural fields
(`type/streamId/eventType/variations/options/composite/datasource`) are
immutable. **Root caveat:** an app/shared token cannot create a top-level
root stream (`parentId: null` → `forbidden`, even with `manage`); if your
custom item needs a brand-new root, request it in the **initial** OAuth
permissions with a `defaultName` so it is provisioned on grant (see §5b /
the accesses notes). Parent custom items under an existing root to avoid
this entirely.

**Then propose it upstream.** A working custom itemDef is the same shape as
a core data-model definition, so it doubles as a ready-made proposal:
validate it against the data-model schemas and submit it to grow the core
model — file at https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues with
your itemDef attached. This is how the catalogue grows: every app that
needs a new observation should contribute it back rather than re-inventing
it privately.

How each interface implements it:

| Interface | Lookup                                          | Spec read                              | Write                                                  |
|-----------|-------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| MCP       | `search_items(query)`                           | `get_item(key)`                        | `create_event` / `import_batch`                        |
| hds-lib   | `getHDSModel().itemsDefs.forKey(key)`            | `item.eventTypes`, `item.label`, …     | `connection.api([{method:'events.create', params:…}])` |
| REST      | `GET https://model.datasafe.dev/pack.json`      | inspect `pack.items[key]`              | `POST /events` (see §6)                                 |

For variations-only items in code, prefer `item.eventTemplate()` — it
returns `{ streamIds: [canonical], type: eventTypes[0] }`, picking the
first variation automatically. Override only when the user requested a
specific unit.

Discovering the item **key** without the MCP: browse
https://model-browser.datasafe.dev (human) or scan the keys of
`pack.items` (machine) — keys are kebab-case and descriptive
(`body-weight`, `body-temperature-basal`, `cervical-fluid-observation`).

---

## 4. How to use HDS — pick one of three

**Agents: scope the project before installing anything.** The MCP server
is only the right surface when the *agent itself* must read/write the
user's HDS data interactively (process a folder, log values, summarize
streams — Flow "process my data"). If the user is **building an app**
(web page, Node program, bridge), the deliverable's code talks to HDS via
`hds-lib` (§4b) and **no MCP install is needed** — don't add one. REST
(§4c/§6) is the fallback for environments where neither fits. When the
user's goal is unclear, ask before installing.

### 4a. The MCP server (AI agent clients: Claude Desktop, ChatGPT, …)

Repo: https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-mcp-js · Transport: stdio ·
Auth: Pryv poll-mode OAuth (no callback URL needed).

**Install in Claude Desktop.** Installs straight from GitHub via `npx` —
no clone needed (the npm-registry name `hds-mcp` is not claimed yet):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "hds": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "github:healthdatasafe/hds-mcp-js"]
    }
  }
}
```

Restart the client fully (⌘Q on macOS) so it picks up the new server. The
first launch can take up to a minute while `npx` downloads and builds;
later launches reuse the cache. Developers working from a source clone can
point at the built output instead (`npm install` builds it):
`{ "command": "node", "args": ["/absolute/path/to/hds-mcp-js/dist/index.js"] }`
— re-run `npm run build` after editing `src/`.

**Tool catalogue (v0.1.0, all tier=essential):** `connect`,
`list_streams`, `get_events`, `search_items`, `get_item`, `create_event`,
`import_batch`. (Naming: tool names are these bare strings; prose like
`hds.connect` just prefixes the server alias from the client config —
`"hds"` in the snippet above.) Defaults: `connect` targets the `demo`
host, requests **all streams** (`streamId: "*"`) at `contribute` level
(read + write events — covers the pilot). Pass `host: "prod"` for
production; `level: "read"` for a read-only session; per-stream scoping
is not yet supported. `create_event` and `import_batch` refuse on
production unless the session was opened with
`connect({ host: "prod", enableWrites: true })` — pass `enableWrites`
ONLY when the user explicitly asked to write to their real production
account, never on your own initiative.

Full per-tool descriptions, error shapes, itemDef-first priming rules,
and extension guide live in
[`hds-mcp-js/AGENTS.md`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-mcp-js/blob/main/AGENTS.md).
The canonical tool descriptions an agent sees at runtime are in
[`hds-mcp-js/src/server.ts`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-mcp-js/blob/main/src/server.ts).

### 4b. `hds-lib` — build a browser app or Node program

Always use [`hds-lib`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js) (not
the raw `pryv` SDK) when the deliverable is anything you're going to
build — a single HTML page, a React app, a Node script, a CLI. `hds-lib`
bundles a patched copy of Pryv internally and **re-exports it** as
`HDSLib.pryv` (and `HDSLib.cmc`), so anything you would have done with raw
`pryv` is reachable through the same global. There is no scenario in an
HDS app where loading raw `pryv` in addition to `hds-lib` is correct —
loading both gives you two copies of the SDK in the same page.

Pryv publishes its own agent reference covering the API surface behind
`HDSLib.pryv`: https://pryv.github.io/llms-full.txt (index:
https://pryv.github.io/llms.txt) — `Connection`, `api()` batching,
`getEventsStreamed`, events with attachments, high-frequency events,
`Browser.setupAuth` customization; exhaustive lib usage in the
`pryv/lib-js` README (https://github.com/pryv/lib-js#readme). CAVEAT:
Pryv's docs describe standalone use and will tell you to
`npm install pryv` — in an HDS app, never do that: everything is already
reachable via `HDSLib.pryv`, and HDS-level rules (itemDef-first, §3) still
apply on top. **Pryv's reference is `streamId`/`eventType`-native — it has
no item concept, so its examples show literal stream ids and types. Do NOT
copy those literals into `streams.create` payloads or `events.get` filters;
in HDS those values must come from the model (§3), never be hand-written.
Use Pryv's docs for the API *mechanics* (batching, streamed reads,
attachments), not for what streams/types to use.**

`hds-lib` wraps Pryv with: the HDS data-model singleton (`initHDSModel`,
`getHDSModel`), item-by-key lookup, `HDSService` (service-info + auth
helpers), app-template scaffolding for common patient/bridge/viewer roles,
localisation, settings, preferred-display, reminders, converters.

**Install — always via a bundler, never via CDN script tag.**

`hds-lib` is published as a git-URL npm package:

```bash
npm install git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js.git
```

Then `import` it from your bundled app:

```js
import * as HDSLib from 'hds-lib';
```

Use any bundler (Vite, Astro, Next, Webpack). For a from-scratch page, the
fastest path is Vite:

```bash
npm create vite@latest my-hds-app -- --template vanilla
cd my-hds-app
npm install
npm install git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js.git
```

A built dev server (e.g. `npm run dev` → http://localhost:5173) is what
you'll front with `backloop.dev` (§4b-i) to get the HTTPS origin Pryv OAuth
requires.

> **Do not** load `hds-lib.js` from a CDN inside a plain HTML file
> (`file://` or `http://localhost`). That recipe looks tempting but fails
> the moment OAuth runs — Pryv refuses non-HTTPS origins, and the gh-pages
> bundle (`https://healthdatasafe.github.io/hds-lib-js/hds-lib.js`) is
> there for the project's own demo pages, not as a supported consumer
> entry point. Always install + bundle.

**Canonical provision→write→read pattern** — what every agent should reach
for. Drive all three off the item keys your app uses; never hand-write a
stream tree or `streamId`/`eventType` strings (see §3):

```js
const APP_ITEM_KEYS = ['body-weight', 'body-temperature-basal'];
const model = HDSLib.getHDSModel();

// 1. PROVISION — streams must pre-exist; they do NOT auto-vivify on events.create.
//    StreamsAutoCreate creates the whole tree (parents first) and is idempotent.
await HDSLib.toolkit.StreamsAutoCreate.attachToConnection(connection).ensureExistsForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS);

// 2. WRITE — eventTemplate() gives { streamIds, type }; picks the first variation for you.
const tmpl = model.itemsDefs.forKey('body-weight').eventTemplate();
await connection.api([{ method: 'events.create', params: { ...tmpl, content: 72, time: epochSeconds } }]);

// 3. READ — derive the streams filter from the SAME set. A single unknown stream in the
//    filter fails the WHOLE events.get (returns zero events), so create the full tree first.
const streamIds = model.streams.getNecessaryListForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS, { knowExistingStreamsIds: [] }).map(s => s.id);
const events = await connection.api([{ method: 'events.get', params: { streams: streamIds } }]);
```

`HDSItemDef` exposes more than `eventTemplate()` — `eventTypes`, `label`,
`description`, `repeatable`, `isDeprecated`, raw `data`, etc. For a custom
observation HDS has no item for, register your own itemDef via
`initHDSModel({ overload })` and keep using this exact pattern (see §3).

**Other high-value helpers** (full enumeration in the lib's AGENTS.md):

| Helper                                            | What it does                                                                                 |
|---------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `eventToShortText(event)`                         | Single shared event formatter — display events as `"60 Kg"`, `"Aspirin · 100mg"`, etc.       |
| `MonitorScope`                                    | Progressive event loading + WebSocket subscribe — use for live timelines / dashboards.       |
| `computeReminders(item, source)`                  | "What's due now" computation for an item.                                                    |
| `HDSSettings`                                     | Per-user settings (locales, theme, timezone, date format, unit system).                       |
| `getPreferredInput(item, ctx)` / `getPreferredDisplay(event, ctx)` | Unit-system-aware input + display preferences.                                     |
| `appTemplates.*`                                  | `AppManagingAccount` / `AppClientAccount` / `Collector` / `CollectorClient` — the legal/consent shape every HDS app uses. |

If you need a low-level Pryv API the HDS layer doesn't wrap, reach it
via `HDSLib.pryv.*` — never load a second `<script>` tag.

**Full reference** — flat public-API index, conventions, gotchas, and
read-order for the docs site:
[`hds-lib-js/AGENTS.md`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js/blob/main/AGENTS.md).
Generated API docs: <https://healthdatasafe.github.io/hds-lib-js/>.

### 4b-companions. Companion libraries (build on top of `hds-lib`)

`hds-lib` is the foundation. When the app needs more (form UIs, specialised
visualisations, a backend bridge), reach for one of these. All three are
git-URL npm packages (none are on the npm registry) and expect a bundler
or Node project — **no CDN script tags**.

| Library                                                                                | Use when                                                                                  | Install                                                                          | Agent docs                                                                                                  |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **[`hds-forms-js`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-forms-js)**                   | Building a React UI that captures canonical HDS events. Key exports: `<HDSFormField>`, `<HDSFormSection>`, `<EntryList>`, `schemaFor`, `prefillFromEvents`, `matchEventsToItemDefs`, `formDataToEventBatch`, `<DatasetSearch>`, `<ItemSearchPicker>`. | `git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-forms-js.git`                          | [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-forms-js/blob/main/AGENTS.md)                              |
| **[`hds-feminine-cycle-ui`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-feminine-cycle-ui)** | Showing cervical-fluid / cycle-tracking events as FEMM / Billings / Creighton / Mira chart cells. Key exports: `<RepresentationCell>`, `<CervixPositionMarker>`, `composeCellInput`, `detectFertilityWindow`, `femmSpec` / `billingsSpec` / `creightonSpec` / `miraSpec`. Peer-dep: `react@^19`. | `git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-feminine-cycle-ui.git`                | [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-feminine-cycle-ui/blob/main/AGENTS.md)                     |
| **[`lib-bridge-js`](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/lib-bridge-js)**                 | Building a backend service that syncs a partner API into HDS. Key exports: `PluginBridge` base class, `startCluster`, `createBridgeApp`, `initBoiler`, `getLogger`. | `git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/lib-bridge-js.git`                        | [AGENTS.md](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/lib-bridge-js/blob/main/AGENTS.md) + [INSTALL.md](https://github.com/healthdatasafe/lib-bridge-js/blob/main/INSTALL.md) |

Reference bridges using `lib-bridge-js`: `bridge-chartneo`, `bridge-mira`,
`bridge-femm`, `bridge-redcap`, `bridge-cycles-files` — look at any for
the canonical service shape.

### 4b-i. Developing with Claude Code (the IDE/CLI)

If the user is iterating on an HDS app **inside Claude Code** (rather than
asking a hosted agent over chat), the runtime has constraints that change
what's possible:

- Claude Code's sandbox runs commands and dev servers locally, but **only
  localhost / 127.0.0.1 binds are reachable by the agent.** The agent can't
  open or proxy to arbitrary remote hosts.
- Opening the HTML file directly (`file://…`) does **not** work for the
  Pryv sign-in flow: the OAuth handshake refuses plain `http://` and
  `file://` origins. It needs **real HTTPS**.
- That means a "just open the HTML file in your browser" recipe is a
  dead-end inside Claude Code. The user has to run an actual dev server
  *and* expose it over a publicly-trusted HTTPS hostname.

The supported recipe:

1. **Install Node.js first.** This is non-negotiable for Claude-Code-based
   HDS development. Use the official LTS installer at https://nodejs.org —
   green "LTS" button, no terminal needed. Skip `nvm` / Homebrew unless
   already in use.
2. **Run a dev server on localhost.** Any static server works for a single
   HTML page (`npx serve`, `npx http-server`, Vite, Astro, …). Pick a port,
   e.g. `4322`.
3. **Use [backloop.dev](https://backloop.dev) to give it real HTTPS.**
   backloop ships a wildcard public cert for `*.backloop.dev` and a tiny
   gateway that proxies `https://<name>.backloop.dev:7854` → your
   `localhost:<port>`. With it, your dev page at
   `https://myapp.backloop.dev:7854/` is OAuth-compatible.
   Docs + source: https://backloop.dev · https://github.com/perki/backloop.dev

Minimal setup:

```bash
npm install -g backloop.dev               # one-time
backloop.dev --config=gateway.json        # equals form — see note below
```

The flag form is `--config=<path>` with an `=` sign and no space. The
CLI's `--help` does not advertise it; a bare positional arg is treated as
a *directory to serve*, not as a JSON config path, so the space form
silently fails.

`gateway.json`:

```json
{
  "port": 7854,
  "hostnames": {
    "myapp": { "proxy": "http://localhost:4322" }
  }
}
```

Then your HTML page from §5b loads at `https://myapp.backloop.dev:7854/`,
which the Pryv auth flow accepts. The agent can also fetch this URL from
inside Claude Code (it's locally proxied through the sandbox-friendly
gateway).

**Port collisions.** Port 7854 is the default but is **not** reserved.
Another backloop instance, a parallel project, or any process already
bound to 7854 will make the gateway fail to start. Pick a different port
in that case — anything free (e.g. 7855, 7856, 8854). Change **two
things together**:

1. The `"port"` field in `gateway.json`.
2. The `:7854` suffix in the URLs you use — e.g. `https://myapp.backloop.dev:7855/`.

The `*.backloop.dev` DNS resolves the same regardless of port; the cert is
also wildcard. Parallel workspaces should pre-pick distinct ports so they
never have to debug "the OAuth popup goes to the wrong app" — that
symptom is almost always a port shared across instances.

If you skip Node.js and backloop, the only HDS path that still works in
Claude Code is **MCP-driven** (§4a) — the agent uses `hds-mcp` to read and
write the user's data without building a browser app at all.

### 4c. REST API (other languages / debugging)

Use REST directly only when you cannot reach for `hds-lib` (Python / Go /
curl script, low-level debugging). For any JavaScript context, use
`hds-lib` instead.

The flow:

```
GET service-info  →  OAuth (gets apiEndpoint)  →  POST /events (with token)
```

**Step 1 — discover the API host:**

```
GET https://demo.datasafe.dev/reg/service/info
```

Returns (trimmed):

```json
{
  "api": "https://demo.datasafe.dev/{username}/",
  "access": "https://demo.datasafe.dev/reg/access/",
  "assets": {
    "hds-model": "https://model.datasafe.dev/pack.json"
  }
}
```

The `api` field is a URL template. Substitute the user's username (returned
by OAuth) to get the API host, e.g. `https://demo.datasafe.dev/jane/`.

**Step 2 — apiEndpoint:**

OAuth is Pryv's **auth-request** flow: POST to the `access` URL from
service-info (with `requestingAppId` + `requestedPermissions` in the
body) → response `status: "NEED_SIGNIN"` with an `authUrl` (give it to
the user) and a `poll` URL → poll until `status` becomes `ACCEPTED`
(carries `apiEndpoint` + `username`) or `REFUSED`. Full working code:
§5c. The result is an **apiEndpoint** for the signed-in user:

```
https://cklm9z6yp00031a3v5kxz9q3m@demo.datasafe.dev/jane/
```

The token is in the URL's userinfo segment (before `@`). The host portion
after `@` is the API host from step 1.

**Step 3 — authentication:**

The token goes in the **`Authorization`** request header, **raw** — no
`Bearer ` prefix, no `Token ` prefix:

```
Authorization: cklm9z6yp00031a3v5kxz9q3m
```

Strip the userinfo from the apiEndpoint before using its host as the
request URL. Never log the apiEndpoint verbatim — it is a bearer
credential.

**Step 4 — POST /events:**

```
POST https://demo.datasafe.dev/jane/events
Authorization: cklm9z6yp00031a3v5kxz9q3m
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "streamIds": ["body-temperature-basal"],
  "type": "temperature/c",
  "content": 36.6,
  "time": 1780300000
}
```

Response (200 OK):

```json
{
  "event": {
    "id": "ckm12z9yp00041a3v8q1ry7d9",
    "streamIds": ["body-temperature-basal"],
    "streamId": "body-temperature-basal",
    "type": "temperature/c",
    "content": 36.6,
    "time": 1780300000,
    "created": 1780305421.487,
    "modified": 1780305421.487
  },
  "meta": { "apiVersion": "…", "serverTime": 1780305421.487 }
}
```

Error responses are non-2xx with shape `{ error: { id, message } }`:

```
HTTP 400
{ "error": { "id": "invalid-parameters-format",
             "message": "type is required" } }
```

**Other endpoints:**

| Method | Path             | Purpose                                  |
|--------|------------------|------------------------------------------|
| GET    | `/streams`       | List streams (tree)                      |
| GET    | `/events`        | Query events (filter via query string)   |
| POST   | `/events`        | Create one event                         |
| PUT    | `/events/{id}`   | Update an event (fix a wrong entry)      |
| DELETE | `/events/{id}`   | Trash an event (second DELETE erases)    |
| POST   | `/`              | Batch call (RPC-style, many ops at once) |
| GET    | `/access-info`   | Inspect the connected access             |

Full Pryv API reference: https://pryv.github.io/reference/. The HDS service
speaks the same protocol — HDS layers the data model on top.

---

## 5. Worked examples

### 5a. MCP — pilot: fertility-charts folder → HDS

> *"Sign me in to HDS demo, then process the folder at `~/fertility-2026/`
> and import every reading."*

```
1. hds.connect()                       → browser OAuth on demo
2. (agent's filesystem tool)            reads the folder
3. (agent's vision tool)                OCRs scanned BBT sheets
4. (agent's code interpreter)           parses Excel
5. For each distinct data type:
     hds.search_items("basal body temperature")
       → top match: body-temperature-basal
     hds.get_item("body-temperature-basal")
       → streamId: "body-temperature-basal",
         eventType: "temperature/c", type: "number"
6. hds.import_batch({ events: [
     { streamIds: ["body-temperature-basal"],
       type: "temperature/c",
       content: 36.6,
       time: <epoch-seconds> },
     ...
   ]})
7. hds.get_events({ streams: ["body-temperature-basal"], limit: 5 })
     → confirms the upload
```

The HDS MCP itself does not ship filesystem I/O or OCR — those are
delegated to the agent client's other tools. The pilot only works
end-to-end if filesystem + vision are available in the client.

### 5b. `hds-lib` — minimal Vite app: sign-in + log a weight

A small Vite project that signs the user in to HDS demo and logs a
body-weight event. Three files. Run via `npm run dev`, then front with
`backloop.dev` to get the HTTPS origin Pryv OAuth needs.

**Bootstrap:**

```bash
npm create vite@latest hds-hello -- --template vanilla
cd hds-hello
npm install
npm install git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js.git
```

**`index.html`** (replace the file Vite generated):

```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>HDS hello</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>HDS hello</h1>
  <span id="login-button">Loading…</span>
  <div id="signed-in" style="display:none; margin-top:1em">
    <p>Signed in. Item: <code id="item-info"></code></p>
    <form id="log-form">
      <input id="weight" type="number" step="0.1" placeholder="Weight in kg" required>
      <button type="submit">Log weight</button>
    </form>
    <pre id="result"></pre>
  </div>
  <script type="module" src="/main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>
```

**`main.js`** (replace `counter.js` / `main.js` from the template):

```js
import * as HDSLib from 'hds-lib';

const SERVICE_INFO_URL = 'https://demo.datasafe.dev/reg/service/info';

HDSLib.settings.setServiceInfoURL(SERVICE_INFO_URL);
HDSLib.settings.setPreferredLocales(['en']);

let connection = null;
let item = null;

// setupAuth() replaces the <span> with a Pryv-styled sign-in button.
// The popup opens to demo.datasafe.dev; the user signs in there.
HDSLib.pryv.Browser.setupAuth({
  authRequest: {
    requestingAppId: 'hds-hello',
    requestedPermissions: [
      // Ask only for what the app needs. body-weight is the canonical
      // streamId — discover keys at model-browser.datasafe.dev, via the
      // MCP's search_items, or by scanning pack.json items.
      { streamId: 'body-weight', defaultName: 'Body weight', level: 'manage' }
    ],
    returnURL: 'self#'
  },
  onStateChange: async (state) => {
    if (state.id === HDSLib.pryv.Browser.AuthStates.AUTHORIZED) {
      // Fresh-path AUTHORIZED carries state.key, not state.apiEndpoint
      // (pryv@3.5.0+). Resolve via connectFromKey, which returns a working
      // Connection whose apiEndpoint is the token-bearing URL — handle privately.
      // Cookie-autologin AUTHORIZED still carries state.apiEndpoint directly.
      connection = state.key
        ? await HDSLib.pryv.connectFromKey(state.key, SERVICE_INFO_URL)
        : new HDSLib.pryv.Connection(state.apiEndpoint);

      await HDSLib.initHDSModel();
      item = HDSLib.getHDSModel().itemsDefs.forKey('body-weight');

      document.getElementById('item-info').textContent =
        item.label + ' — types: ' + item.eventTypes.join(' / ');
      document.getElementById('signed-in').style.display = 'block';
    }
  },
  spanButtonID: 'login-button'
}, SERVICE_INFO_URL);

document.getElementById('log-form').addEventListener('submit', async (ev) => {
  ev.preventDefault();
  const kg = parseFloat(document.getElementById('weight').value);
  // body-weight is variations-only → eventTemplate() picks mass/kg.
  const tmpl = item.eventTemplate();
  const res = await connection.api([{
    method: 'events.create',
    params: { ...tmpl, content: kg }
  }]);
  document.getElementById('result').textContent =
    JSON.stringify(res, null, 2);
});
```

**Run + expose over HTTPS:**

```bash
npm run dev                          # Vite on http://localhost:5173
# in another terminal:
npx backloop.dev --config=gateway.json
```

The flag form is `--config=<path>` (equals, no space). The CLI's `--help`
under-documents this — `backloop.dev <path>` treats the bare arg as a
directory to serve, not as a config file.

`gateway.json`:

```json
{
  "port": 7855,
  "hostnames": {
    "hello": { "proxy": "http://localhost:5173" }
  }
}
```

Open `https://hello.backloop.dev:7855/` in a browser. The Pryv sign-in
button renders; sign-in opens a popup and returns an apiEndpoint; the form
writes a `body-weight` event using `item.eventTemplate()`.

Port 7855 is chosen here to dodge collision with the canonical default
7854 (often already taken by an existing backloop instance). For the full
port-collision rule, see §4b-i.

Why this shape (vs. a plain HTML file + CDN script tag): Pryv OAuth refuses
non-HTTPS origins, and `file://` / `http://localhost` won't satisfy it.
`backloop.dev` puts a publicly-trusted `*.backloop.dev` cert in front of
your localhost dev server, so OAuth works.

### 5b-multi. `hds-lib` — a real multi-item app (provision → write → read)

§5b logs a single item, so it never has to build a stream tree. **Real apps
use several items across nested streams** — and that's exactly where the
two early HDS apps went wrong (they hand-coded a `STREAM_TREE` constant and
hardcoded `streamId`/`eventType` strings; see §3). Here is the pattern that
keeps everything model-derived. The whole app is keyed off one list:

```js
import * as HDSLib from 'hds-lib';

// The ONLY app-specific declaration: which model items this app uses.
// Streams, parent chains, names, and event shapes are ALL derived from this.
const APP_ITEM_KEYS = [
  'body-weight',
  'body-temperature-basal',
  'cervical-fluid-observation'
];

// ── after sign-in (connection ready) ───────────────────────────────────
await HDSLib.initHDSModel();
const model = HDSLib.getHDSModel();

// 1. PROVISION the full tree ONCE, at sign-in — parents first, idempotent.
//    Create everything the app reads, so the first read can't hit an unknown stream (§7).
//    StreamsAutoCreate does the getNecessaryListForItems → streams.create batch for you and
//    swallows "item-already-exists" — it is what the early apps reimplemented by hand.
await HDSLib.toolkit.StreamsAutoCreate.attachToConnection(connection).ensureExistsForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS);

// 2. WRITE — event shape comes from the item, never hand-written.
async function logItem(itemKey, content, time = Date.now() / 1000) {
  const tmpl = model.itemsDefs.forKey(itemKey).eventTemplate();   // { streamIds, type }
  return connection.api([{ method: 'events.create', params: { ...tmpl, content, time } }]);
}
await logItem('body-weight', 64.2);
await logItem('body-temperature-basal', 36.7);

// 3. READ — the events.get filter is derived from the SAME item set.
//    (If even one listed stream didn't exist, events.get would return ZERO events — §7.)
const streamIds = model.streams.getNecessaryListForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS, { knowExistingStreamsIds: [] }).map(s => s.id);
const history = await connection.api([{ method: 'events.get', params: { streams: streamIds } }]);
```

Adding a new data domain to this app is a **one-line** change: add its item
key to `APP_ITEM_KEYS`. The next sign-in provisions the new streams,
writes use its `eventTemplate()`, and reads pick it up — no tree edit, no
new `streamId`/`eventType` constant. If you need an observation the model
doesn't cover, register a custom itemDef via `initHDSModel({ overload })`
(§3) and add its key here too.

### 5c. `hds-lib` in Node — CLI sign-in with terminal URL

Pattern for any non-browser context (CLI tool, Node script, batch job, an
agent running in Claude Code that wants to act on the user's account
without spawning a browser itself). The script kicks off Pryv's
**poll-mode** auth flow, prints the sign-in URL to stdout, then polls
until the user signs in. **Agents in Claude Code: surface the printed URL
to the user verbatim — they click it in their own browser.**

**Bootstrap:**

```bash
mkdir hds-cli-hello && cd hds-cli-hello
npm init -y
npm install git+https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js.git
```

Make `package.json` ESM by adding `"type": "module"`.

**`index.js`:**

```js
import * as HDSLib from 'hds-lib';

const SERVICE_INFO_URL = 'https://demo.datasafe.dev/reg/service/info';
const APP_ID = 'hds-cli-hello';
const PERMISSIONS = [
  { streamId: 'body-weight', defaultName: 'Body weight', level: 'manage' }
];

HDSLib.settings.setServiceInfoURL(SERVICE_INFO_URL);

// 1. Discover the access endpoint, kick off poll-mode auth.
//    POST to the bare `info.access` URL — the appId is carried in the
//    body as `requestingAppId`. (POSTing to `info.access + appId` is the
//    sign-in page's *result-reporting* endpoint and will 400.)
const info = await (await fetch(SERVICE_INFO_URL)).json();
const initRes = await fetch(info.access, {
  method: 'POST',
  headers: { 'content-type': 'application/json' },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    requestingAppId: APP_ID,
    requestedPermissions: PERMISSIONS,
    languageCode: 'en',
    returnURL: 'self'
  })
});
const init = await initRes.json();
if (init.status !== 'NEED_SIGNIN') {
  throw new Error(`unexpected auth status: ${init.status}`);
}

// 2. Print the URL for the user to click.
//    Do not auto-open a browser — let the human (or the wrapping agent)
//    decide. In Claude Code, surface this URL verbatim to the user.
console.log('\n  Open this URL in a browser and sign in to HDS:\n');
console.log(`    ${init.authUrl ?? init.url}\n`);
console.log('  Waiting for sign-in…');

// 3. Poll until accepted, refused, or timeout (5 min).
//    The field is snake_case in the real response (`poll_rate_ms`).
const pollUrl = init.poll;
const intervalMs = init.poll_rate_ms ?? 1000;
const deadline = Date.now() + 5 * 60 * 1000;

let apiEndpoint = null;
let username = null;
while (Date.now() < deadline) {
  await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, intervalMs));
  const data = await (await fetch(pollUrl)).json();
  if (data.status === 'ACCEPTED') {
    apiEndpoint = data.apiEndpoint;
    username = data.username;
    break;
  }
  if (data.status === 'REFUSED') {
    throw new Error(`auth refused: ${data.message ?? 'no reason given'}`);
  }
  process.stdout.write('.');
}
if (!apiEndpoint) throw new Error('auth timed out');

console.log(`\n  Signed in as ${username}\n`);

// 4. Now use hds-lib normally — model + connection.
await HDSLib.initHDSModel();
const item = HDSLib.getHDSModel().itemsDefs.forKey('body-weight');
const connection = new HDSLib.pryv.Connection(apiEndpoint);

const res = await connection.api([{
  method: 'events.create',
  params: { ...item.eventTemplate(), content: 72 }
}]);
console.log('Wrote event:', JSON.stringify(res, null, 2));
```

**Run:**

```bash
node index.js
```

Output (real-shape — the auth host is `demo-account.datasafe.dev`, query
param is `poll=`):

```
  Open this URL in a browser and sign in to HDS:

    https://demo-account.datasafe.dev/access/access.html?lang=en&key=…&poll=…

  Waiting for sign-in…
....
  Signed in as jane

Wrote event: { "results": [ { "event": { "id": "ckm…", … } } ], … }
```

Verified live against demo.datasafe.dev: the `NEED_SIGNIN` response keys
are `status, code, key, requestingAppId, requestedPermissions, authUrl,
url, poll, poll_rate_ms, lang, returnURL, returnUrl, oauthState,
clientData, serviceInfo`. (`authUrl` and `url` are duplicates — either
works.)

Verified end-to-end against demo.datasafe.dev (2026-06-10): a real
sign-in round-trip confirmed the `ACCEPTED`-branch fields
(`data.apiEndpoint`, `data.username`) and the final
`connection.api([{ method: 'events.create', … }])` write — the script
above ran unmodified and the event was independently re-read via the
API.

The sign-in URL is real HTTPS served by HDS — no `backloop.dev` needed
here, because the script never serves anything itself; it only **consumes**
the user's sign-in completion via the poll URL.

### 5d. REST — minimal curl (after OAuth)

Once you have an apiEndpoint, the smallest useful sequence:

```bash
TOKEN='cklm9z6yp00031a3v5kxz9q3m'
HOST='https://demo.datasafe.dev/jane'

# Log a basal body temperature
curl -s "$HOST/events" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "streamIds": ["body-temperature-basal"],
    "type": "temperature/c",
    "content": 36.6
  }'

# Read it back
curl -s "$HOST/events?streams=body-temperature-basal&limit=5" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN"
```

### 5e. `hds-lib` — coach ↔ patient sharing (mint → consume → list → revoke)

The canonical pattern for one user (a **patient**) to grant another user (a
**coach**) scoped, revocable, read-only access to part of their account.
Built on Pryv's `accesses.*` API; verified live. The patient must have
already provisioned the streams being shared (§3) so the coach's reads
don't hit the §7 read trap.

```js
// ── PATIENT side: mint a shared access scoped to specific roots, read-only ──
const shareRes = await patientConnection.api([{
  method: 'accesses.create',
  params: {
    type: 'shared',
    name: 'coach-jane-2026',
    permissions: [
      { streamId: 'body',      level: 'read' },
      { streamId: 'fertility', level: 'read' },
      { streamId: 'symptom',   level: 'read' }
    ],
    clientData: { 'app:role': 'coach-share' }   // tag so you can filter your own shares later
  }
}]);
// The created access carries a ready-to-use apiEndpoint: https://<token>@<host>/<username>/
const coachApiEndpoint = shareRes[0].access.apiEndpoint;
// (equivalently: HDSLib.pryv.utils.buildAPIEndpoint(serviceInfo, token) from the owner endpoint + token.)
// Hand coachApiEndpoint to the coach over a trusted channel — it embeds a bearer token.

// ── COACH side: consume it ──
const coachConnection = new HDSLib.pryv.Connection(coachApiEndpoint);
const events = await coachConnection.api([{ method: 'events.get', params: { streams: ['body', 'fertility', 'symptom'] } }]);
// read-only: the coach can render the patient's chart but not write.

// ── PATIENT side: list shares (filter to ones this app minted) ──
const all = await patientConnection.api([{ method: 'accesses.get', params: {} }]);
const myShares = all[0].accesses.filter(a => a.clientData?.['app:role'] === 'coach-share');

// ── PATIENT side: revoke ──
await patientConnection.api([{ method: 'accesses.delete', params: { id: myShares[0].id } }]);
// After revoke the coach's token returns HTTP 403 `invalid-access-token` immediately.
```

Notes: per-stream `read` scoping means the coach sees only the shared
roots. An **app token can self-revoke** via `accesses.delete` on its own
access, and that **cascades to child shared accesses** it created — useful
for the GAP-007 re-authorization migration (§7).

---

## 6. Production guardrails

- **Default host is demo.** `connect` with no `host` argument targets
  `demo.datasafe.dev`. `host: "prod"` (or a full service-info URL) targets
  production.
- **MCP: in-memory tokens only.** The MCP holds the apiEndpoint for the
  lifetime of the server process. Restart → re-OAuth. No disk, no keychain.
- **Browser apps: session persistence is allowed — choose deliberately.**
  Note `pryv.Browser.setupAuth` **already persists a cookie**
  (`pryv-libjs-<appId>`) and auto-restores from it, so the blanket "in-memory
  only" advice does not match the library's own behavior — and that
  auto-restore is inconsistent in practice (some reloads land back on
  sign-in). Pick one model explicitly rather than relying on the default:
  cookie (the lib's default), `localStorage` (survives reload, app-managed),
  or in-memory (re-OAuth every reload). The apiEndpoint is a bearer
  credential reachable by XSS wherever it is stored — never ship it to a
  third party, never log it. **Always validate a restored token before
  trusting it:** call `getAccessInfo` (a revoked/expired token returns
  403 `invalid-access-token`); on failure clear the stored value and fall
  back to sign-in. Validating on restore also avoids a stale cookie showing
  a deceptive "signed-in" state.
- **Prod writes are a per-session user opt-in.** `create_event` and
  `import_batch` refuse on production unless the session was opened with
  `connect({ host: "prod", enableWrites: true })`. Agents: pass
  `enableWrites` only when the user explicitly asked to write to their
  production account — the user then confirms on the consent screen. The
  unlock dies with the session (client restart → back to safe).
  `HDS_MCP_PROD_WRITES_ENABLED=true` in the MCP's environment is a
  global override (operator use). Reads on prod work unconditionally.
- **Token scrubbing (MCP only).** All log, error, and telemetry paths in
  the MCP run through a scrubber that redacts the token portion of any
  apiEndpoint URL (`https://TOKEN@host` → `https://***@host`). `hds-lib`
  does **not** scrub for you — code built on it must never log, print, or
  persist an apiEndpoint (it is a bearer credential).
- **Development permissions: wide while developing, narrow when done.**
  While a project is in development (against a demo account), request
  `manage` on `*` streams — you don't yet know which streams the project
  ends up touching, and the wide scope lets you create/reshape streams
  without friction. **Once development is done, shrink the requested
  access to the minimal set the finished flow actually uses** — the
  specific streams, at the lowest sufficient level
  (`read` < `contribute` < `manage`). Wide scopes are a sandbox
  convenience, never the end state for anything touching a real account.

---

## 7. Error shapes

### MCP errors (verbatim, as of v0.1.0)

| Trigger                                       | Message                                                                                  |
|-----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| No `connect` called yet                       | ``Not connected. Run the `hds.connect` tool first.``                                     |
| Unknown host alias                            | ``Unrecognised host: X. Use 'demo', 'prod', or a full service-info URL.``                |
| Prod write without session opt-in             | ``Writes to the production HDS host are disabled for this session. If the user explicitly asked to write to their production account, re-run connect with { host: 'prod', enableWrites: true } ...`` |
| OAuth refused                                 | ``auth refused: <reason>``                                                               |
| OAuth poll timed out                          | ``auth timed out — please re-run hds.connect``                                           |
| Pryv API (GET) error                          | ``Pryv <path> failed: <upstream message>``                                               |
| Pryv API (POST) error                         | ``Pryv POST <path> failed: <upstream message>``                                          |
| Pryv batch error                              | ``Pryv batch failed: <upstream message>``                                                |
| Item key not found in pack.json               | ``No HDS item found for key "X".`` (with optional ``Did you mean one of: ...?`` hint)    |

### Pryv API (HDS backend) status codes (verified live on demo, 2026-06-10)

| Status | Typical error id            | Meaning                                                   |
|--------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| 400    | `invalid-parameters-format` | Bad request (missing/ill-typed field)                     |
| 400    | `unknown-referenced-resource` | A referenced stream does not exist — see the read trap below |
| 401    | `invalid-access-token`      | **Missing** Authorization header                          |
| 403    | `invalid-access-token` / `forbidden` | **Invalid** token, access level too low (e.g. write with a `read` access — re-auth with `contribute`), **or** attempting to create a top-level root stream with an app/shared token — see the root note below |
| 404    | `unknown-resource`          | Unknown user/resource                                     |
| 5xx    | `unexpected-error`          | Server-side error                                         |

Match on the error `id`, not only the status — body shape:
`{ "error": { "id": "<error-id>", "message": "..." } }`.

**Sharp edge — the read trap (`events.get` + an unknown stream).** If the
`streams:` filter of an `events.get` lists **any** stream that doesn't exist
on the account, the call returns a single `unknown-referenced-resource`
error and **zero** events — it does NOT ignore the unknown stream or return
a partial result. So an app that queries its full stream set reads *nothing*
until every one of those streams exists, even ones that already have data.
**Fix:** provision the complete stream tree once at sign-in
(`StreamsAutoCreate.ensureExistsForItems(APP_ITEM_KEYS)`) before the first
read, and derive both the create set and the read filter from the model
(§3). This single behaviour is what made both early apps show "no data"
despite successful writes.

**Sharp edge — roots and `defaultName` provisioning.** (1) An app/shared
token **cannot** create a top-level root stream: `streams.create` with
`parentId: null` returns `forbidden` even with `manage` on that exact
streamId. (2) Requesting a permission with a `defaultName` provisions that
root — but **only on the INITIAL authorization**. Adding a new root
permission to an already-granted access (the consent screen's "Update
permissions" path) updates permissions but does **not** create the new
root, so writes to it fail with `unknown-referenced-resource`. Ship every
root your app will ever need in the first OAuth scope, or migrate existing
users by revoking + re-authorizing (an app token can self-revoke via
`accesses.delete`, which cascades to its child shared accesses).

---

## 8. What HDS does NOT do

- It is not a clinical EHR. It is a personal record under the user's
  control. Don't present HDS-retrieved values as medical advice.
- The MCP does not store credentials. Each session re-OAuths.
- The MCP and `hds-lib` do not invent items. If the user's data does not
  match any item in `pack.json`, surface that to the user before writing —
  then either define a custom itemDef via `initHDSModel({ overload })` and
  propose it upstream (§3), or pick a fitting event-type; never hand-write
  loose `streamId`/`eventType` strings or dump structured data into `note/txt`.
- `hds-forms-js` (the React form-renderer library) is not currently
  CDN-loadable as a standalone bundle. For forms UIs, build a Vite/React
  app and consume the npm package directly. The single-HTML pattern uses
  `hds-lib` only.

---

## 9. Where to look next

| Topic                            | URL                                                   |
|----------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| HDS site                         | https://datasafe.dev                                  |
| Foundation                       | https://www.healthdatasafe.org                        |
| Developer docs                   | https://docs.datasafe.dev/developers/                 |
| Agents site (humans first)       | https://agents.datasafe.dev                           |
| Agent bootstrap instructions     | https://agents.datasafe.dev/bootstrap.txt             |
| Feedback (GitHub issues)         | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/site-agents/issues  |
| Data model (machine-readable)    | https://model.datasafe.dev/pack.json                  |
| Data model browser (human)       | https://model-browser.datasafe.dev                    |
| `hds-mcp-js` source              | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-mcp-js          |
| `hds-lib-js` source              | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-lib-js          |
| `hds-lib-js` docs                | https://healthdatasafe.github.io/hds-lib-js/          |
| `hds-forms-js` source            | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-forms-js        |
| `hds-feminine-cycle-ui` source   | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/hds-feminine-cycle-ui |
| `lib-bridge-js` source           | https://github.com/healthdatasafe/lib-bridge-js       |
| Pryv agent reference (read for `HDSLib.pryv` internals; ignore its `npm install pryv` — see §4b) | https://pryv.github.io/llms-full.txt |
| Pryv agent index                 | https://pryv.github.io/llms.txt                       |
| Pryv API reference (low-level)   | https://pryv.github.io/reference/                     |
| `pryv` JS lib docs (the API `HDSLib.pryv` re-exports — doc only, never install raw) | https://github.com/pryv/lib-js#readme |
| Data in Pryv (best concept walk-through: streams, events, accesses) | https://pryv.github.io/data-in-pryv/ |
| Pryv concepts overview           | https://pryv.github.io/concepts/                      |
| Pryv event-types catalogue       | https://pryv.github.io/event-types/                   |
| `backloop.dev` (local HTTPS)     | https://backloop.dev · https://github.com/perki/backloop.dev |
| Patient app (demo)               | https://demo-app.datasafe.dev                         |
| Doctor dashboard (demo)          | https://demo-doctor-dashboard.datasafe.dev            |

---

End of llms-full.txt
